mnemonics.title: DDL Commands Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: CRAD: Create, Rename, Alter, Drop - These are your structure-changing commands!

mnemonics.title: Constraint Types
mnemonics.content: PUN NC (PUNishment Not Allowed): Primary key, UNique, NOT NULL, Check - the column rules!

mnemonics.title: Data Type Categories
mnemonics.content: NumbErS CHAracters DAtes BINary - NEDB helps remember: Numeric, Character, Date, Binary types

acronyms.usage: SQL commands that define database structure: CREATE, ALTER, DROP, TRUNCATE, RENAME
Used for creating/modifying database objects like tables and indexes
Fixed-length string data type that pads shorter values with spaces
Variable-length string that stores only actual data without padding
Stores large text data up to several gigabytes
Stores binary data like images, audio, video files
Commands for data manipulation: SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE (different from DDL)
Unique identifier for each row, cannot be NULL
Reference to primary key in another table, enforces referential integrity

visualTips.visualization: Think of a table as an Excel spreadsheet with named columns (headers), each having a specific data type (text, number, date). Constraints are like rules written under each column header.
Primary Key is like your Aadhaar/ID number - uniquely identifies you. Foreign Key is like your parent's ID that links you to them in another table.
CHAR is like a fixed parking slot - always reserves full space. VARCHAR is like flexible storage - takes only what's needed.
DROP = burn the house down (everything gone), TRUNCATE = empty the house but keep walls, DELETE = remove items one by one from the house.
Think of foreign key as a chain linking two tables - the chain ensures you can't have child records without valid parent records.

analogies.analogy: Like a structured spreadsheet with column headers (field definitions), data types (format), and validation rules (constraints). Each row is a record.
Like rules in a form: Required fields (NOT NULL), Unique ID (PRIMARY KEY), Checkboxes/ranges (CHECK), References to other forms (FOREIGN KEY).
Like a book index at the back - helps find information quickly without scanning every page, but takes extra space to maintain.
Like renovating a building - you can add new rooms (columns), change room sizes (modify), or remove rooms (drop).
Like containers in a kitchen - bowls for liquids (DATE), boxes for solids (CHAR), containers for bulk items (BLOB). Each holds different things.
